# List requests

**Connector:** [SDP - Service Desk](../README.md) (`sdp-service-desk`) ¬∑ **Action index:** [all actions](README.md)
**Endpoint:** `GET /api/v3/requests` ¬∑ **Operation ID:** `ListRequests`

Pages through service desk requests, optionally sorted and filtered server-side. This is the workhorse read: prefer a tight `search_criteria` over pulling pages and filtering in the flow.

## Parameters

| Designer label | Name | Where | Type | Required |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Input Data (JSON) | `input_data` | query string | string | no |
| Accept | `Accept` | header | string | yes |

### Input Data (JSON) (`input_data`)

Lists service desk requests with pagination, sorting, and search criteria. Full format documented in the payload section below.

### Accept (`Accept`)

Fixed technical header, preset to `application/vnd.manageengine.sdp.v3+json` ‚Äî it selects
SDP's V3 media type (and, for worklogs, the current V3 worklog format). It is marked
*internal*, so the designer hides it from makers; you'll only see it in the connector's code
view. Leave it as is, including `required: true` ‚Äî Power Platform silently drops optional
internal headers (see the repo's [LESSONS.md](../../../LESSONS.md)).

## The `input_data` envelope ‚Äî every variation

This action sends `input_data` as a **URL query parameter** holding a `list_info` JSON
envelope. The connector URL-encodes it for you ‚Äî just paste JSON. Leave the box empty (or keep
the preset default) for SDP's defaults.

### Formatting rules

- **One line of valid JSON.** Paste the whole envelope as a single line. JSON syntax only:
  **double quotes** around every key and string value, no trailing commas, no comments.
- **IDs are strings.** SDP returns its numeric IDs as JSON strings; send them the same way ‚Äî
  `{"id":"1234"}`.
- **Reference fields take an object**, addressed by any unique key you have:
  `{"name":"High"}`, `{"id":"301"}`, or ‚Äî for people ‚Äî `{"email_id":"tech@company.com"}`.
- **Dates and times are epoch milliseconds (UTC).** Payload date fields are objects like
  `{"value":"1735689600000"}` (2025-01-01T00:00:00Z); inside `search_criteria` the value is the
  bare digit string.
- **Building the JSON in a flow:** dynamic content is spliced in as plain text, so the result
  must still be valid JSON. For free text that may contain quotes/newlines, sanitize first
  (e.g. `replace(<text>, '"', '\"')` after doubling backslashes), or assemble the payload with
  a **Compose**/`json()` step and `string()` it into the parameter.

### Keys `list_info` supports

Verified against the official on-prem `input_data` reference:

| Key | Type | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `row_count` | int | rows to return ‚Äî **maximum 100 per call** |
| `start_index` | int | index of the first row, for paging |
| `sort_field` | string | field to sort by (a field you see in the response) |
| `sort_order` | string | `"asc"` or `"desc"` |
| `get_total_count` | bool | adds `total_count` to the response `list_info` (default `false`) |
| `search_criteria` | object / array | filter conditions ‚Äî see the variations below |
| `fields_required` | array | trim the response to just these fields |

The response echoes a `list_info` with `has_more_rows` (and `total_count` if you asked) ‚Äî
loop with a growing `start_index` while `has_more_rows` is `true`.

### Variation 1 ‚Äî plain paging

```json
{"list_info":{"row_count":25,"start_index":1}}
```

Page 2 is `"start_index":26`, and so on. Add `"get_total_count":true` on the first call to
size the loop:

```json
{"list_info":{"row_count":25,"start_index":1,"get_total_count":true}}
```

### Variation 2 ‚Äî sorting

```json
{"list_info":{"row_count":25,"sort_field":"created_time","sort_order":"desc"}}
```


### Variation 3 ‚Äî filtering with `search_criteria`

One criterion filters the list. Module examples (shipped default uses the first):

```json
{"list_info":{"row_count":10,"start_index":1,"get_total_count":true,"search_criteria":{"field":"status.name","condition":"is","value":"Open"}}}
```

```json
{"list_info":{"row_count":50,"search_criteria":{"field":"requester.email_id","condition":"is","value":"user@company.com"}}}
```

```json
{"list_info":{"row_count":50,"search_criteria":{"field":"subject","condition":"contains","value":"printer"}}}
```

### The condition catalogue

All conditions supported by `search_criteria` (official on-prem reference), with their
shorthand aliases:

| Condition | Aliases | Typical use |
|---|---|---|
| `is` | `=`, `eq` | exact match (strings, numbers, booleans, `{"id":...}` objects) |
| `is not` | `!=`, `neq` | exclusion |
| `greater than` | `gt` | numbers, epoch-ms dates |
| `greater or equal` | `gte` | numbers, epoch-ms dates |
| `lesser than` | `lt` | numbers, epoch-ms dates |
| `lesser or equal` | `lte` | numbers, epoch-ms dates |
| `between` / `not between` | ‚Äî | ranges; pass the two bounds in `values` |
| `starts with` / `ends with` | ‚Äî | text |
| `contains` / `not contains` | ‚Äî | text |

Field paths use dot notation for sub-fields (`status.name`, `priority.name`,
`requester.email_id`). Two equivalent filter shapes work: a dotted field with a plain value
(`"field":"status.name","condition":"is","value":"Open"`) or the parent field with an object
value (`"field":"status","condition":"is","value":{"name":"Open"}`).

### Variation 4 ‚Äî multiple values (IN-style)

Use `values` (plural) with a list:

```json
{"list_info":{"row_count":50,"search_criteria":{"field":"priority.name","condition":"is","values":["High","Urgent"]}}}
```

### Variation 5 ‚Äî combining criteria with AND / OR

`search_criteria` can be an **array**; every entry after the first carries a
`logical_operator` (`"AND"` or `"OR"`):

```json
{"list_info":{"search_criteria":[
  {"field":"status.name","condition":"is","value":"Open"},
  {"field":"priority.name","condition":"is","value":"High","logical_operator":"AND"}
]}}
```

### Variation 6 ‚Äî grouped (nested) criteria with `children`

`children` nests sub-criteria under a parent, i.e. parentheses. This means
*status is Open AND (created after 2025-01-01)*:

```json
{"list_info":{"search_criteria":{
  "field":"status.name","condition":"is","value":"Open",
  "children":[{"field":"created_time","condition":"greater than","value":"1735689600000","logical_operator":"AND"}]
}}}
```

### Variation 7 ‚Äî date windows

Dates in criteria are **epoch milliseconds as digit strings** (UTC). A window uses `between`
with both bounds in `values` ‚Äî this is 2025-01-01 to 2025-07-01:

```json
{"list_info":{"search_criteria":{"field":"created_time","condition":"between","values":["1735689600000","1751328000000"]}}}
```

Power Automate tip for "now" in epoch ms:
`div(sub(ticks(utcNow()), ticks('1970-01-01T00:00:00Z')), 10000)`.

### Variation 8 ‚Äî only the fields you need

```json
{"list_info":{"row_count":100,"fields_required":["id","subject","status"]}}
```


The parameter's preset default is:

```json
{"list_info":{"row_count":10,"start_index":1,"get_total_count":true,"search_criteria":{"field":"status.name","condition":"is","value":"Open"}}}
```

## Authentication

Nothing to do per action: the connection you created for this connector sends the technician
API key on every call as the `authtoken` header (a secured connection parameter ‚Äî it never
lives in a flow or in the definition). Generate keys in SDP under *Admin ‚Üí Technicians ‚Üí
Generate API Key*, ideally for a least-privilege integration technician. The repo
[README](../../../README.md) covers key scoping and the HTTPS-only rule.

## Response

Success (200) returns the `RequestListEnvelope` shape:

- `response_status` ‚Äî ResponseStatus object
- `list_info` ‚Äî ListInfo object
- `requests` ‚Äî array ‚Äî items: `id`, `subject`, `status`, `priority`, `created_time`, `requester`, `technician`, `group`, `site`, `due_by_time`, `is_overdue`

Each entry in `requests` carries `id`, `subject`, `status.name`, `priority.name`, `requester`, `technician`, `group`, `site`, `created_time`/`due_by_time` (`display_value` + epoch-ms `value`) and `is_overdue` ‚Äî `id` is what the Get/Update/Close/Delete actions need.

Success means `response_status.status_code` **2000** ‚Äî check that, not just HTTP 200. One
shape wobble to handle in flows: on **list** successes `response_status` is an **array** of
status objects, on **single-resource and error** responses it's an **object**. The connector
deliberately leaves it untyped; branch on `Array.isArray`-style logic (e.g. Power Automate
`first(...)` when it's an array).

Anything else comes back on the `default` response as the SDP error envelope ‚Äî surface `status_code` and `messages` in your flow's failure branch.

## Notes, limits & verification

- Read operation, **live-verified** on the public demo (HTTP 200, status_code 2000).
